posts.caption:
WEST’S SILENT OUTCRY
“It’s basically like the inside of my brain. It tells the story from when I was about 19 to now. It explores five different emotions, each just as intense as the others.
It’s called Man’s Ruin.” — Westislonely
Following the release of her new single ‘Bad Dog’ and ahead of a new EP due this July, Westislonely presents music rooted in the spirit of punk. Through striking black-and-white imagery, her music embodies the energy of rock, expressing her inner emotions with raw honesty.
The Westislonely full editorial will be available on our website soon.

posts.caption:
Fragrance is an invisible memory that lasts the longest. It becomes part of my identity and a subtle presence in everyday life and memory. And here is a fragrance brand that finds beauty in what exists around us — @flanerfragrances.
FLÂNER is inspired by the city, drawing from real observations rather than imagined escapes: rain on tram tracks, burnt timber, spring flowers pushing through concrete. But as the world moves faster, finding beauty in everyday surroundings — especially in busy cities — becomes more difficult.
Even now, when AI has become part of our daily lives, it is becoming hard to maintain pure curiosity, have deep conversations with others, and experience new things in person.
In the midst of these changes, FLÂNER is built on the belief that even in a fast-paced world, human experience still matters. Here, you’re encouraged to slow down, explore scents at your own pace, and find a fragrance that truly suits you. This luxury niche fragrance line is curated into nine scents, ranging from the brand’s signature Manhattan Cherry (inspired by the speakeasies of Manhattan) to Amber Narcotique (inspired by the addictive pull of love), the editor’s pick — a sensual and seductive fragrance.
The space is quiet and unhurried, designed so you can focus on scent without distraction. As you discover a scent that resonates with you, personal memories and sensations naturally begin to form and stay with you.
Discover your scent at Redchurch 52, Shoreditch.
Words by Julie Jeong
Edited by Josephine Jiang
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷@a2z_fashionmag
#FLÂNER #NicheFrgrance

posts.caption:
On 19 June, @Hermès officially opened its new flagship on @bondstreet in London. The new boutique, which had already attracted significant attention and anticipation ahead of its opening, did not disappoint. The Maison, created by connecting six buildings, is organised around a spiral staircase designed by British architect @officialnormanfoster, spanning five floors and 55 spaces. Within this setting, Hermès’ diverse collections are presented in one place, from fashion to beauty, homeware, and objects beyond. More than 500 artworks and sculptures are displayed throughout the space, turning it into an immersive luxury lifestyle experience rather than a conventional fashion house.
Rooted in craftsmanship and a quiet luxury ethos, #Hermès presents a distinctive sense of colour and wit that resonates naturally with British sensibilities. This is why the expression “Hermès is the most British of French houses” feels particularly fitting. The interior design and furnishings of each category space reflect this identity in full.
Masion means ‘Home,’ and a home is both a place of comfort and a source of inspiration. The Hermès London Maison embodies both qualities. Instead of excessive opulence, it offers the atmosphere of a refined historic British townhouse. Within it, products, art, and space come together to form a Maison that inspires quietly, yet enduringly.
Words by Julie Jeong
Edited by Josephine Jiang
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷 @a2z_fashionmag
#HermesMaison #BondStreet

posts.caption:
Designed by London-based label @avvattev, the lace-up knit has quietly become one of fashion’s cult pieces — spotted across cinema, music and off-duty style, from @paulmescal’s much-discussed cardigan moments to a growing list of celebrity fans. Founded by Bulgarian-born designer and @bafcsm graduate Antonio Vattev, the brand blends London youth culture with references to music, performance and 70s bohemian sensuality.
Swipe to see who has worn it.
Words by Josephine Jiang
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
📷@avvattev, Kristine Kwak

posts.caption:
There was a sweetness to @simonerocha_’s Spring/Summer 2027 Menswear collection – the designer’s first standalone men’s collection. “We wanted to soften a little bit, to make it feel sensitive next to masculinity,” Rocha said of these clothes. Often, her menswear has a boyish, even blokey toughness, cutting through the pretty of her womens. For its solo debut, Rocha countered, “It wasn’t about bringing in a femininity, but a tenderness.” Held in a 17th-century opera house in Florence, it was broderie anglaise boxer shorts, rugby shirts with pie-crust, Sloane-ranger ruffles, and boas flung and flying. ⁠
⁠
Read @alexanderfury’s review at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 #SimoneRocha Spring/Summer 2027 Menswear. Photography by @benjamintayl0r ⁠
posts.alt_text:
Just shy of her 50th birthday, @vincapetersen left behind the life she had built in Ramsgate and moved to the Isle of Skye, where she took on the tenancy of a croft (a traditional Scottish term for a small agricultural plot of land) and began constructing a straw-bale house with the help of friends, students and fellow builders. Documenting it all through her ever-human photography and diary writing, the resulting body of work, Hulala, traces four years of upheaval, reinvention and collective labour. ⁠
⁠
While Hulala emerges from a very different chapter in Petersen’s life, those familiar with her cult 1999 book No System, which chronicled years spent travelling through Europe with rave sound systems, will recognise familiar threads in its ecstatic images taken in nature. But where No System found freedom and collective euphoria in life on the road, Hulala turns its attention to permanence, asking what it means to build a home not only as a physical structure but as a social one.⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, Petersen speaks with @orlabeee to reflect on reaching a pivotal crossroads at 50, the radical history of Scottish crofting, and the collaborative spirit at the heart of Hulala 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Vinca Peterson: Hulala. Photography by Vinca Peterson

posts.caption:
“Since I started taking images, there’s always been this searching of – or seeking to understand – my environment,” says photographer @kendalwalkerr. ⁠
⁠
Though now based in London, Walker grew up in Utah, and the distinct atmosphere of that mountainous, small town landscape continues to permeate her work. Recurring images of lone figures in empty landscapes foster a pervading feeling of solitude. The textures of the environment are often abrasive or hostile – snow, asphalt, rubble, chainlink fence.⁠
⁠
Curated by the artist’s sister, her latest photo book, Tracks, is composed of photographs taken from the last eight years of her archive, but its origins may date back to an earlier time. “There’s not a distinct starting point for Tracks, it started much before I was conscious of what I was actually making,” she says. Like memory, it’s not chronological. ⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, the photographer speaks with @ohnoitsemilyd about the new book 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Tracks by Kendal Walker⁠

posts.caption:
“It is important to me that the woman is not perceived only through her body, but also through her own gaze,” says @adeljanska, whose large scale paintings of doll-like figures are now on display at @rolandoanselmigalerie gallery in a solo show titled Near Field. “I feel that the gaze largely determines who the woman in the painting is.” ⁠
⁠
Familiar yet strange, Janská’s figures appear to dwell in a different dimension, one that is close yet beyond our reach. Humming with tension, each subject seem to carry the weight of something left unsaid. Janská’s women watch and wait for some kind of acknowledgement, their muted chalky pastel hues only accentuating the sense of disquiet.⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, Janská speaks to @lydunishka about her latest show 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Adéla Janská: Near Field. Artwork by Adéla Janská⁠

posts.caption:
A young man peers out from a diminutive frame, only 2 x 2, small enough to hold in the palm of one’s hand. In the medium’s expected fashion, he looks directly at us, his expression sober. Under the gaze of his attentive daughter, this young man transforms. He is a superhero or a god, and always a muse.⁠
⁠
These images are contained in Passports, a series of over 300 works by photographer @scarvillek, in which the artist reconfigures her father’s earliest passport photograph. On 11 June, @mack_publishing released a monograph of the series, Scarville’s second with the publisher. ⁠
⁠
“The first iteration of the series was a collage,” says Scarville. She flipped through magazines, looking for “images of other Black and brown people to “infuse the passport photo with [a] multiplicity,” she explains. Working with her father’s image soon became a ritualistic, daily practice. ⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, @aselodie speaks to Scarville about the series 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Keisha Scarville, from Passports (MACK, 2026). Courtesy of MACK and the artist⁠

posts.caption:
Dreamlike, theatrical, irreverent and enticing, @camille_vivier’s work abounds with artistic, cultural and cinematic references. ⁠
⁠
Frequently, these evocations are subtle and layered: a hint of David Lynch, a whisper of Alfred Hitchcock, a sly nod to Cindy Sherman, comic books or Carlo Mollino. Occasionally, they are immediately apparent: her striking, statuesque depictions of women bodybuilders and bold, beautifully staged still lifes, for instance, pay direct homage to Robert Mapplethorpe.⁠
⁠
It was this particular connection that Paris’ Maison Européenne de la Photographie (@mep.paris) was keen to explore when its team first approached Vivier about hosting an exhibition of her work. “It’s a sort of retrospective, but not really because I’m not that old,” the photographer tells @daisyaecw. “More a selection of different works from among my body of work, looking at the key subjects and patterns like the body or anthropomorphic shapes, shape in general … ”⁠
⁠
Read the interview at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 by #CamilleVivier © ⁠

posts.caption:
“Sometimes I love to overdo everything and go crazy. I love bright colours: pink, orange, sunset colours, or the colours of beautiful flowers,” says @inkawilliams as a self-described minimalist, with maximalist tendencies. The French-Australian model is a vision of effortless beauty as she embodies the youthful spirit of the Chanel Rouge Coco Hydra Gloss, its frosted finishes, vivid colours and high-shine, hypnotising tubes.
Through the link in bio, see the beauty behind Inka Williams’ salty skin, flushed cheeks and reflective lips.
#InkaWilliams wears @chanel.beauty Rouge Coco Hydra Gloss and Summer Makeup Collection on the June 2026 digital cover of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.
Words: @itsalexjduffy
Photography: @jamie_.green
Makeup: @victoriabaron using @chanel.beauty
Hair: @darrensummors
Styling: @rachelwayman
Fashion assistant: @kaileeewaller
Production director: @rebeccamooreproductions
Art director: @_cathryn.s.z
Digital director: @ariellekatos
posts.alt_text:
While Bali is ingrained in @inkawilliams’ life and sense of self, the beach is something she had to leave to love. “My mum used to force us to go to the beach. I remember hating it. I’m a Virgo . . . all the sand on my hands and my feet when I would go back to the car,” she tells BAZAAR. “Eventually, when I grew up and moved to London, I realised how much I need the beach. I don’t mind if it’s cold, but I need the sun wherever I am. I need nature. If I go too long without being in nature, I start to lose my mind.”

At the link in bio, Inka Williams details her connection to Bali and the daily rituals which keep her grounded far from home. 

#InkaWilliams wears @chanel.beauty Rouge Coco Hydra Gloss and Summer Makeup Collection on the June 2026 digital cover of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.

posts.caption:
When home is somewhere between the island of Bali, Melbourne, Los Angeles, London and Paris, ‘home’ is a feeling @inkawilliams finds within herself. The Australian-born model was raised in Bali, where her childhood home remains an oasis from bustling city streets and long days on set. “I just love being at home,” says Williams. “The garden is just so beautiful. I feel like I’m living in a painting. Everything [in Bali] has developed around the house, but my house is still a bubble of green. I never want to leave home.”
Through the link in bio, the model and creative multihyphenate shares more on feeling at home across the world and with her love.
#InkaWilliams wears @chanel.beauty Rouge Coco Hydra Gloss and Summer Makeup Collection on the June 2026 digital cover of Harper’s BAZAAR Australia/New Zealand.

posts.alt_text:
While Bali is ingrained in @inkawilliams’ life and sense of self, the beach is something she had to leave to love. “My mum used to force us to go to the beach. I remember hating it. I’m a Virgo . . . all the sand on my hands and my feet when I would go back to the car,” she tells BAZAAR. “Eventually, when I grew up and moved to London, I realised how much I need the beach. I don’t mind if it’s cold, but I need the sun wherever I am. I need nature. If I go too long without being in nature, I start to lose my mind.”

At the link in bio, Inka Williams details her connection to Bali and the daily rituals which keep her grounded far from home.

Sixteen years after its debut, the Valentino Garavani Rockstud remains one of fashion’s most enduring silhouettes. For Spring/Summer 2026/27, @maisonvalentino creative director Alessandro Michele revisits the iconic style through a fresh lens, introducing strappy sandals, flats and pumps in two versatile heel heights. Arriving in a palette that spans azure blue and light green to timeless black and white, the collection balances house heritage with a new sense of ease.
Visit the link in bio to discover the full story. #ValentinoGaravani #Rockstud.

posts.caption:
Harper’s Bazaar India digital cover star, Maye Musk (@mayemusk), started modelling as a teenager. She stopped at 22 to get married and raise three children, then picked it back up at 28—an age the industry considered, at the time, well past the point of no return. So she built her nutrition practice in parallel. By 31, she was in high demand as a model and continued part-time; in her 60s, she was doing high fashion. “If someone had told me as a teenager that I would be in Delhi for a top fashion magazine at 78, I would have thought they were crazy,” she says. “Yet, here I am, working alongside a very creative team.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)
Interview: Geetika Sachdev (@geetikasachdev)
Photographer: Soujit Das (@soujit.das)
Stylist: Gopalika Virmani (@gopalikavirmani)
Cover Design: Mandeep Singh Khokhar (@mandy_khokhar19)
Editorial Coordinator: Shalini Kanojia (@shalinikanojia)
Make-up Artist: Itika Chugh (@fashionmakeupbyitikachugh)
Hair Artist: Umang Artist (@umang.artist), at Anima Creatives (@animacreatives)
Style Assistant: Grace Soni (@grace__soni_)
Maye Musk is wearing the Ethereal top with an abstract coral reef–inspired belt with sculptural organic textures, by Richa Khemka (@richakhemkalabel)
#HarpersBazaarIndia #MayeMusk
posts.alt_text:
From discussing masculinity and mindset to family, fashion, and finding comfort in authenticity, Jasprit Bumrah (@jaspritb1) keeps it refreshingly real in conversation with digital editor Sonal Ved (@sonalved). Speaking about everything from his love for fragrances and watches to switching off with family time, the ace cricketer proves that personal style is less about trends and more about individuality. He also shares why collaborating with Ralph Lauren Fragrances (@ralphlaurenfragrances) felt like a natural fit for his personal style and personality. Whether on the field or off duty, his approach remains grounded, thoughtful and deeply personal.

Tap the link in bio to watch the full video.

Styling: @eshaamiin1 

#BazaarIndia #HarpersBazaarIndia

posts.caption:
In an industry where surnames are inherited currencies, Bazaar India’s April-May cover star, Tabu (@tabutiful), has operated for over three decades on a mononym. To understand her career is to understand what it meant to say yes to things the industry was not ready for and to have the instinct to recognise those things as worthy, even when no template existed for them. But she refuses to call it rebellion. “It was simply a reflection of who I am. The roles I chose, the decisions I made—they came from some similar place throughout my life. Some part of me has been completely unchanged,” she insists.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.
Editor: Rasna Bhasin (@rasnabhasin)
Interview: Malika Halder (@malikahalder)
Photographer: Soujit Das (@soujit.das)

From airport chases to dramatic confessions in the rain, pop culture spent years convincing us that love had to feel cinematic. But the way we think about relationships is changing. Timing, compatibility, and emotional maturity now matter just as much as chemistry. Maybe love today is less about finding someone flawless and more about finding someone willing to navigate real life with you.
Tap the link in bio to read on.

Adrian Appiolaza (@adrianappiolaza) is exiting @Moschino after over two years as creative director.
The decision was mutual, the Italian fashion house said in a statement on Friday.
“I would like to thank Adrian Appiolaza for his significant contribution to the development of Moschino over the past two years and wish him every success in his future professional endeavours,” said Massimo Ferretti, executive chairman of Moschino owner Aeffe.
A new creative director will be announced “in due course,” the brand said.
Read more on the announcement #linkinbio

posts.caption:
Fashion’s Mona Lisa is Canadian model Kirsten Owen as she was photographed in the mid-’80s by Paolo Roversi in his friend Romeo Gigli’s designs. Owen was 16 when she first sat for Roversi, but her haunted, haunting composure belies her age. She is quite the match of Leonardo’s Renaissance original. “My angel of light,” Roversi calls her now. “Much better than a muse, she was really unique, the first model who taught me that fashion photography could touch another dimension.”
It’s that other dimension that gives Roversi’s work its power. Imagine if someone had a camera in Byzantium. “I think my work is very spiritual, because I’m not satisfied only by reality. I’m always trying to touch another energy.”
Read the full interview where Roversi opens up to Tim Blanks (@timblanks) about the soul, doubt and paradox that powers his work. #linkinbio
📷 @roversi

Ismail Hussein Sidi Ahmed (aka Miles)

posts.caption:
Cider was built for virality. When TikTok took off during pandemic lockdowns, the founders of the Los Angeles-based online retailer saw an opportunity in creating an online store that responded to trends as quickly as they arose on the platform.
The E-tailer is now generating mid-nine figures in annual sales without attracting the same sort of public ire as competitors. But pulling ahead in the saturated online fast fashion market and getting to the $1 billion mark will require new levers to be pulled.
BoF's Diana Pearl (@dianaspearl) speaks to co-founder and chief fashion officer, Fenco Lin, about the company's quest to turn viral success into a lasting global brand. Read more #linkinbio
📷 @shopcider

posts.alt_text:
Our cover star opts for cinema that sparks conversations, takes pride in her individuality, and fiercely protects her mental peace. “Today, there’s clarity in my choices. I’m shedding old versions of myself to make space for newer, riskier, more interesting ones,” she says, unfiltered, unassuming and refreshingly candid. Bhumi Satish Pednekkar welcomes Brides Today (@bhumisatishpednekkar) into her world of stylish experiments, socially resonant movies, sustainable choices, and the legacy she hopes to leave behind. 

Check out the complete cover story in the link in bio.

posts.caption:
this is such a great example of culture-led marketing done right. sports, fashion and storytelling came together completely organically, and the brand was smart enough to recognise the moment and run with it. it’s proof that people connect with narratives far more than products. for smaller brands especially, creating and capitalising on these cultural moments can be far more powerful than any traditional campaign. great work by the team on turning a simple coincidence into a story people genuinely cared about.
#jordynwoods #knicks #marketing
posts.alt_text: ummm what just happened?

posts.caption:
yes, the image is eye-catching, but how one perceives it is completely shaped by their own mindset and gaze.
this also reminds me of the recent situation involving olivia rodrigo, where her baby doll dresses were unnecessarily sexualised and she clapped back perfectly. just as beauty lies in the eye of the beholder, so does perceived inappropriateness.
there is nothing fundamentally wrong with these images. yes, they’re shot in a more editorial way that might make you look twice, but if you see something inherently wrong with the idea of a woman being with her child, in this case a doll lmao, that’s entirely your projection, not the image itself.

posts.caption:
Simone Rocha made her menswear show debut at @pittiuomo_official in Florence on Thursday evening. While the temperature was high outside the Teatro Della Pergola, inside, the scene was cool and collected as guests took to the stage to find their seats in the round. This was a show inspired and fueled by the notion of performance. Rocha’s skill in celebrating classical menswear codes—tailoring, shirting, and Oxford brogues—were insouciantly fused with the romantic flourishes that she has built her brand DNA on. (Pussybow blouses, floral embroidery anglaise shirts, pinafores, ballet flats, and aprons!) @simonerocha_ called it “a grand tour,” and one that celebrated every age of man through her light touch and romantic lens.
“There is knowing innocence to proportions that nod to different ages of man—childhood, adolescence, adulthood,” read the show notes. “The space in which we convene also leaves its trace on this collection, a reaction to place and time. Floral patterns are drawn from the linings of costume trunks, a Florentine scene becomes a souvenir print, airs of the theatre universe animate silhouettes with a languid swagger, within this ancient city mythologized through art and literature.”
posts.alt_text: Nekisha Durrett's "Hem of Heaven" is one of the final artworks that has been installed at the Obama Presidential Center before its long-awaited opening. The 24-foot-tall sculpture, covered in midnight blue tiles, carries deep historical weight.

posts.caption:
Former first daughters Malia and Sasha Obama made a special appearance on Thursday, stepping out alongside their parents—former President @barackobama and former First Lady @michelleobama —for the official dedication ceremony for the Obama Presidential Center in Chicago. The two sisters each showed off their personal style for the rare outing while channeling a bit of their mother’s signature way of dressing, too. Malia, 27, wore a smart gray blazer paired with a mini skirt while Sasha, 25, opted for a textured off-the-shoulder, cream-colored knit set from @laurenmanoogian.
📸: Kent Nishimura/AFP/Getty Images / Win McNamee/Getty Images /Rebecca Wright/CNN
posts.alt_text: Nekisha Durrett's "Hem of Heaven" is one of the final artworks that has been installed at the Obama Presidential Center before its long-awaited opening. The 24-foot-tall sculpture, covered in midnight blue tiles, carries deep historical weight.

posts.caption:
The First Lady of New York City has entered the chat. Artist @ramaduwaji appeared alongside her husband, Mayor Zohran Mamdani, in lower Manhattan today to join in on the celebratory ticker-tape parade thrown in honor of the @nyknicks who won their first NBA championship in 53 years last week. For the special outing, Duwaji wore a multi-colored, one-shoulder dress made of bootleg Knicks graphic t-shirts from designer @missclairesullivan. In an extra festive touch, she also wore a pair of statement orange, basketball inspired pom-pom earrings.
📸: Getty Images
posts.alt_text: Nekisha Durrett's "Hem of Heaven" is one of the final artworks that has been installed at the Obama Presidential Center before its long-awaited opening. The 24-foot-tall sculpture, covered in midnight blue tiles, carries deep historical weight.

posts.caption:
@michelleobama has long demonstrated a knack for conveying messages through her clothing, and her outfit on Tuesday poignantly exemplified her mastery of this skill.
For an appearance on stage at the new Obama Presidential Center in Chicago, the former first lady wore a pencil skirt adorned with a large portrait of her late mother. The custom @acnestudios design was a tribute to Marian Robinson who died in 2024, aged 86.
The sepia-toned skirt had been specially commissioned with the help of her longtime stylist @meredithkoop, and featured one of Obama's favorite pictures of her mother.
The design riffed on a runway look from Acne's Fall-Winter 2026 collection, which featured a similar skirt with an image of a young man by the Dutch artist @paulkooiker.
The styling was also similar. Like the model in the Paris show, Obama wore the skirt with a thin belt, chocolate brown T-shirt and slingback pumps.
📸: Pablo Martinez Monsivais/Getty Images
posts.alt_text: Nekisha Durrett's "Hem of Heaven" is one of the final artworks that has been installed at the Obama Presidential Center before its long-awaited opening. The 24-foot-tall sculpture, covered in midnight blue tiles, carries deep historical weight.

posts.caption: Have you missed her? Zendaya made a chic return to the red carpet on Monday, stepping out for the first official photo call for her upcoming sure-to-be blockbuster “Spider Man: Brand New Day” alongside her partner and costar, Tom Holland. The pair coordinated in matching black, spider-centric looks with Zendaya wearing an asymmetrical dress from @christiancowan and Holland in custom @jacquemus.
posts.alt_text: Nekisha Durrett's "Hem of Heaven" is one of the final artworks that has been installed at the Obama Presidential Center before its long-awaited opening. The 24-foot-tall sculpture, covered in midnight blue tiles, carries deep historical weight.

posts.caption:
For a mile-long stretch of Bushwick, Brooklyn, a sea of red, white and blue floods the streets.
One corner boasts a DIY speaker setup blasting old-school reggaeton courtesy of Tego Calderón and Plan B, another plays throwback salsa from Héctor Lavoe and El Gran Combo.
Booming horns pulse through the air; vendors pour “piragua” snow cones and serve watermelon and coconut ice cream to those looking to cool down; and elders kick back in personal lawn chairs taking in the festivities, which include surprise appearances from @nycmayor Zohran Mamdani and @nyknicks star (and newly crowned NBA champion) Jose Alvarado (@therealgta.)
Despite the heat and humidity, a palpable sense of pride ripples through the crowd. This is, after all, what fuels New York City’s Puerto Rican Day Parade weekend, year after year.
The Bushwick event coincides with a long-running parade that has seen Puerto Rican New Yorkers (or “Nuyoricans”) take to Manhattan’s streets every second Sunday in June since 1958.
“There’re more Puerto Ricans in the US than there are on the island right now. Now that I’ve been here in the city for 13 years, there’s a lot of longing to be back home,” says Monica Santos Gil, the founder of New York-based handbag brand Santos by Monica and a passionate festival-goer. “For those who maybe feel less connected to the culture of the island, the parade is such an incredible opportunity for Puerto Ricans to come together, celebrate that connection and feel close to home.”
📹 : CNN
📸: David Dee Delgado for CNN
posts.alt_text: Nekisha Durrett's "Hem of Heaven" is one of the final artworks that has been installed at the Obama Presidential Center before its long-awaited opening. The 24-foot-tall sculpture, covered in midnight blue tiles, carries deep historical weight.

posts.caption:
@luxpascal_ is one to watch—and not for long. After a breakout year that included a Netflix film, an Off-Broadway debut in “Richard II,” and a casting announcement in Tom Ford’s next feature film, she’s stepping into a moment that’s been building for years.
For our #RenewalIssue, our cover star reflects on rejection, momentum, and what comes next.

There are influencers, and then there’s @madelineargy. Since 2021, the “Pretty Lonesome” host has made eight million strangers feel like her closest friends without even trying. Whether she’s giving advice on toxic friendships or crashing out over Jane Goodall, it’s clear that Argy’s got range—and so does her closet.

Coveteur went inside the closet of her LA home and found rows of candy-colored @chanelofficial bags on one wall and thrifted tanks from @thredup and @depop on the other.

Tap the link in bio for our latest #ClosetTour to step inside her world, from the breakup that led to her most-treasured boots to why she’ll defend backpacks until the very end.

posts.caption:
Long before boho returned to fashion’s agenda, Gucci Fall/Winter 2008 was already doing it at its most luxurious. Under Frida Giannini, the house embraced a vision of glamour that was rich, romantic, and unapologetically excessive.
Velvet jackets, flowing dresses, jewel tones, ornate embellishments, fur accents, and layers of gold jewelry moved down the runway with effortless confidence. The collection drew from the spirit of the jet set, blending seventies bohemianism with old-world opulence and rock-and-roll glamour.
What makes the collection feel so relevant today is its refusal to be understated. Every look celebrates individuality, fantasy, and the pleasure of dressing up. Nothing is quiet, nothing is restrained, and that’s precisely what makes it so compelling.
In an era increasingly defined by minimalism, Gucci FW08 offered the opposite: a wardrobe built on drama, texture, and personality. It wasn’t just about clothes—it was about creating a character.
More than fifteen years later, the collection remains a reminder that fashion can be maximalist, emotional, and glamorous all at once. Perhaps that’s why so many designers and consumers are looking back at this era again. Sometimes, more really is more.
Collection: Gucci Fall/Winter 2008 Womenswear
Creative Director: Frida Giannini
Fashion House: Gucci
Presented during: Milan Fashion Week
posts.alt_text:
That question sits at the heart of „Brilliantly British“, a British Vogue editorial that transformed some of fashion’s biggest names into a cast of polished rebels. Featuring Kate Moss alongside a new generation of models, the story plays with the visual language of punk, leather, tartan, studs, ripped fabrics, heavy boots, and deliberately undone beauty looks, while placing it firmly within the world of high fashion.

It’s a contradiction that makes the editorial so compelling. Punk was born as a rejection of convention, yet here it is reinterpreted through the pages of one of fashion’s most influential magazines. What once existed outside the system becomes part of it. Rebellion becomes aesthetic. Anti-fashion becomes fashion.

Rather than documenting punk as it was, Brilliantly British explores punk as a cultural symbol—one that continues to be borrowed, reinvented, and commercialized decades after its emergence. The result is less about authenticity and more about the enduring appeal of attitude. Because whether fashion truly understands punk or not, it has never stopped being fascinated by it.

And perhaps that’s why the editorial still resonates today. It raises a question that remains surprisingly relevant: if everyone can dress like a punk, what does being punk actually mean?

Credits

Editorial: Brilliantly British
Magazine: British Vogue
Photographer: Tim Walker
Stylist/Fashion Editor: Lucinda Chambers
Models: Kate Moss, Lily Donaldson, Georgia May Jagger, Cara Delevingne, Edie Campbell, Karen Elson, Stella Tennant, Jourdan Dunn and others
Hair: Sam McKnight
Makeup: Val Garland

posts.caption:
More than twenty years later, Gucci Spring/Summer 2002 by Tom Ford still feels like the way to dress for summer.
The collection wasn’t about doing too much. It was about confidence. Unbuttoned shirts, lightweight tailoring, perfectly cut trousers and just the right amount of skin.
Tom Ford understood that the most compelling style often comes from ease rather than excess. The silhouettes were sharp but never restrictive, balancing sensuality with sophistication in a way that felt effortless.
At a time when menswear is once again embracing a more relaxed attitude, the collection feels surprisingly current. No gimmicks, no overstyling, just great clothes worn with confidence.
If there’s one menswear collection worth revisiting before summer, it’s this one.

posts.caption:
Before the term “It girl” was recycled into a new trend every few months, Lily-Rose Depp had already become the internet’s latest obsession.
At just 15, she seemed to appear everywhere at once. Not through carefully orchestrated branding or viral content, but through a kind of effortless presence that felt increasingly rare. As the daughter of Johnny Depp and Vanessa Paradis, she arrived with undeniable visibility, yet what fascinated people wasn’t her last name, it was the way she carried it.
Her early Instagram felt refreshingly ordinary. Grainy selfies, photos with friends, school moments, blurry snapshots, and the occasional glimpse into a world most people could only imagine. There was no obvious strategy behind it. No polished influencer aesthetic. No attempt to constantly explain herself.
At the same time, she was quietly stepping into fashion. By her mid-teens, she had become one of Chanel’s youngest ambassadors, frequently photographed alongside Karl Lagerfeld. Yet despite the industry’s attention, she never felt inaccessible. She existed in a space between celebrity and normality, glamour and teenage awkwardness.
That balance made her endlessly compelling.
Long before TikTok trends and carefully curated personal brands became the norm, Lily-Rose represented a new kind of cool: understated, slightly mysterious, and completely unforced. People weren’t just interested in what she was doing, they were interested in who she was.
Looking back, it’s easy to see why the internet became so fascinated. Lily-Rose Depp didn’t break the internet because she was trying to. She did it because, for a brief moment, she embodied something social media rarely allows anymore: authenticity.

posts.caption:
Vintage Roberto Cavalli is having a moment again and it couldn’t feel more timely.
For the past few years, fashion has been dominated by restraint. Quiet luxury, clean lines, neutral palettes, and an obsession with looking effortlessly expensive. But lately, the mood has started to shift.
People want personality again.
They want color, drama, prints, embellishment, and clothes that don’t apologize for taking up space. And that’s exactly where Roberto Cavalli’s early 2000s archive comes in.
Long before maximalism became a trend cycle, Cavalli built an entire universe around excess. Animal prints, saturated colors, body-skimming silhouettes, low-rise cuts, and an unapologetically glamorous attitude defined collections that felt designed to be noticed.
Particularly in the early 2000s, Cavalli mastered a kind of jet-set glamour that feels surprisingly relevant today. His dresses weren’t subtle, his prints weren’t restrained, and that was precisely the point.
As fashion slowly moves away from minimalism, a new generation is rediscovering the appeal of clothes with character. Not everything needs to be quiet. Not everything needs to blend in.
Maybe that’s why vintage Cavalli feels so exciting again.
Maybe we don’t want quiet luxury this summer.
Maybe we want Roberto Cavalli.

posts.caption:
Photographed by Tim Walker and guest-edited by John Galliano, the story brings together Galliano and Kate Moss in a theatrical world inspired by the Ballets Russes performance, and fantasy. Throughout the editorial, Moss transforms into a series of characters, moving between reality and imagination with the ease that has made her one of fashion’s most enduring muses.
Walker’s signature visual language, romantic, surreal, and richly cinematic, elevates the story beyond fashion imagery. Rather than focusing solely on the clothes, Fantasia celebrates creativity itself, exploring the relationship between designer and muse, art and performance, fantasy and reality.
The result is an editorial that feels timeless: a meeting of some of fashion’s most influential creative figures, united by a shared love of storytelling and transformation. More than a decade later, Fantasia remains a reminder of fashion’s ability to create entire worlds through image alone.
Editorial: Fantasia
Magazine: British Vogue, December 2013
Photographer: Tim Walker
Guest Fashion Editor: John Galliano
Fashion Editor/Stylist: Kate Phelan
Model: Kate Moss
Hair: Sam McKnight
Makeup: Sam Bryant
Manicure: Anatole Rainey

posts.caption:
Olivia Rodrigo has a lot of influences. ⁠
⁠
Most recently, in her new Dazed cover interview, she revealed that post-punk bands such as The Cure, New Order, Depeche Mode, and Siouxsie and the Banshees helped shape the sound of you seem pretty sad for a girl so in love. “Hopefully it translated,” she said.⁠
⁠
Her artistry is also informed by books, films, TV shows (special shoutout to Sex and the City) and more. ⁠
⁠
During the course of our conversation, the 23-year-old star shone some light on some of her top cultural picks – swipe ➡️, as we delve into some of them in a little more detail. 🔍️⁠
⁠
✍️ @s_mithereens⁠
⁠
#oliviarodrigo #dazed

posts.caption:
For years, diamond brooches were seen as ceremonial pieces with limited relevance.
That perception is changing.
From red carpet styling and wedding wear to men’s jewellery and multifunctional designs, brooches are re-emerging as one of the most underleveraged categories in fine jewellery retail.
Why this matters for jewellers:
• Offers strong design differentiation
• Appeals to younger, style-conscious buyers
• Works across multiple occasions and wardrobes
• Enhances perceived value through craftsmanship
• Creates fresh storytelling opportunities
The biggest shift? Consumers no longer see brooches as one-dimensional ornaments.
They see them as versatile statement pieces that can be worn on blazers, saris, gowns, bags, hats, and even transformed into pendants or hair accessories.
In a market crowded with rings, earrings, and necklaces, brooches offer retailers a chance to stand out.
Sometimes, the biggest opportunities are hidden in categories the industry has overlooked.
Do you believe diamond brooches are ready for a full-fledged comeback?
Share your thoughts below.
#DiamondWorld #DWMagazine #DiamondBrooch #JewelleryTrends #JewelleryRetail
posts.alt_text:
For years, diamonds were marketed as symbols of eternity.

Botswana just showed the world they can also symbolize movement, achievement, and social impact.

At the 2026 World Athletics Relays, every gold medal featured a traceable natural diamond mined, cut, and polished in Botswana.

That single decision reframed the diamond narrative.

What This Means for the Industry
💎 Provenance is becoming a competitive advantage.

💎 Diamonds can showcase measurable community impact.

💎 Producer nations must retain more value through beneficiation.

💎 Natural diamonds gain strength when linked to authentic stories.

💎 Consumers increasingly reward transparency over mythology.

The Strategic Shift
The future of natural diamonds lies in connecting:
Origin + Ethics + Purpose + Storytelling

Because the most valuable diamond isn’t just beautiful.

It proves where it came from and what it made possible.

As Dr M’zée Fula Ngenge, Chairman of the African Diamond Council, puts it:

“A diamond at its best is not a possession, but a promise.”

What role can diamonds play beyond jewellery?

#NaturalDiamonds #DiamondWorld #DiamondIndustry #DWMagazine #DiamondsWithPurpose

posts.caption:
The future of natural diamonds won’t be won through supply alone.
It will be won through meaning.
ALROSA’s newly approved 2030 promotion strategy signals a major shift in focus — from production and pricing to storytelling, education, transparency and consumer confidence.
The question is no longer:
“Can the industry sell more diamonds?”
It’s:
“Can the industry communicate why they matter?”
💬 What will drive natural diamond demand the most over the next decade?
#DiamondWorld #NaturalDiamonds #ALROSA #DiamondIndustry #DiamondRetail

posts.caption:
Diamonds are among the few objects on Earth that will outlast all of us. That raises a profound question:
Do we ever truly own a diamond?
In her compelling essay, Canada-based visual artist Reena Ahluwalia introduces the Billion-Year Paradox  a philosophical framework that redefines diamonds not as possessions, but as enduring witnesses to human stories.
Key Takeaways for the Jewellery Industry
💎 A natural diamond is a 3-billion-year geological miracle.
💎 Human ownership is temporary. Stewardship is enduring.
💎 In a world of deepfakes and digital noise, diamonds represent authenticity.
💎 Storytelling, provenance, and emotional relevance are becoming the true drivers of value.
💎 The brands that win will be those that connect fleeting moments to timeless meaning.
We never own diamonds. We only borrow their forever.
How does your brand communicate the enduring meaning of natural diamonds?
#NaturalDiamonds #DiamondWorld #JewelleryRetail #DWMagazine

posts.caption:
Yesterday, Andrea Marshall, the designer behind Salon 1884, was accused by Manhattan District Attorney Alvin L. Bragg, Jr. of running a sweatshop in NYC’s Garment District. Marshall faces felony charges of grand larceny, scheme to defraud, and failure to secure payment, as well as a misdemeanor charge for failure to pay wages. She has pleaded not guilty.
“As alleged, Andrea Marshall, the owner of Salon 1884, exploited and stole approximately $54,000 in wages from her employees while selling luxury garments,” the District Attorney said. “She preyed on the significant power imbalance of her employees who relied on the promised wages for housing and basic living expenses, feared retaliation, and lacked the financial resources to pursue legal recourse.”
He went on to describe a recurring cycle in which Marshall would “berate” workers and “scream at them” before periods of non-payment.
“And that’s on top of grueling hours, up to 17 hour days, and up to 100 hour work weeks,” the District Attorney added.
Marshall is alleged to have withheld payment for 924 work hours accrued by nine workers. Not only did she fail to pay them for these hours, but she withheld compensation even after the workers obtained small claims judgments. Text messages show one of the workers begging Marshall for payment, explaining how she needed to send funds to Ecuador for her sick son.
Marshall’s misconduct is not isolated to garment production workers, but extends to contractors and freelancers who worked with Salon 1884 such as “modeling agencies, a pattern maker, and a hair stylist.” She also neglected to make payments to worker’s compensation insurance.
Marshall, despite yesterday’s court proceedings, posted six times on her personal Instagram to promote a Salon 1884 online archive sale. Ironically, Salon 1884’s website touts “exceptional craftsmanship and sustainable practices” and says that garments are “responsibly made with environmentally-conscious materials and manufactured in The United States in New York City’s Garment Center.”
Guess it just goes to show you really can’t assume anything about pricing equating to fair labor in fashion!

posts.caption:
This week, Snap Inc. announced their latest contribution to the deeply flawed and highly polemical world of augmented reality/artificial intelligence glasses. The tech giant is leaning on fashion to sell the $2,195 hardware, tapping photographer Steven Meisel for a sleek launch campaign.
However, photos of Snap Inc. CEO Evan Spiegel wearing the glasses IRL have all but completely derailed any of the cool-factor the campaign may have drummed up.
Snap admits that building a computer into a cool, lightweight pair of glasses is not an easy task. In their press release, they cite the “isolating and cumbersome” nature of tech glasses and headgear that exists on the market today. However, with the ultimate goal of wearability in mind, Snap has utilized “advanced technology found in Boeing 787 Dreamliner windows” for one of several layers of lenses packed into their featherweight Swiss TR90 polymer frames.
In other words, you’ll be lugging a commercial airliner, a whole computer, and some sporty dad goggles on your face all at the same time.
Notable features include interactive AI and overlaying screens. With these tools, Snap boasts that you can “transform almost any place into a workspace.” Because that’s exactly what the people need: A thinner barrier between our professional lives and every single other aspect of our lives.
But the most controversial aspect of this tech in general is the privacy element, especially as it relates to built-in cameras. Meta’s model has been marred with issues relating to its cameras streaming private footage to subcontractors in Kenya, for example. Snap claims that their glasses, “ask clearly before accessing sensitive information, and an LED indicator lights up when recording is taking place,” but Snap does not go into further detail about how the glasses might ask and acquire consent from its wearer, or anyone else for that matter.
All that said, the most pressing question on everyone’s mind is: Will Snap’s glasses allow you to project Snapchat filters onto everyone around you? The answer remains unclear.

Marshall faces felony charges of grand larceny, scheme to defraud, and failure to secure payment, as well as a misdemeanor charge for failure to pay wages. She has pleaded not guilty.

“As alleged, Andrea Marshall, the owner of Salon 1884, exploited and stole approximately $54,000 in wages from her employees while selling luxury garments,” the District Attorney said. “She preyed on the significant power imbalance of her employees who relied on the promised wages for housing and basic living expenses, feared retaliation, and lacked the financial resources to pursue legal recourse.”

He went on to describe a recurring cycle in which Marshall would “berate” workers and “scream at them” before periods of non-payment. 

“And that’s on top of grueling hours, up to 17 hour days, and up to 100 hour work weeks,” the District Attorney added.

posts.caption:
Imagine having a glimpse into Charli XCX’s mind as she was getting ready to launch her ‘Rock Music’ video, or into Paige Buecker’s process as she prepared to step onto the WNBA court. @Coach’s new storytelling platform, &Coach, is collaborating with Gen Z activists, artists, actors, and athletes to do just that.
Launching today, &Coach’s line up of co-authors is stacked with impressive names that resonate with Gen Z in particular, like Charli XCX, PinkPantheress, Malala Yousafzai, Paige Bueckers, Angel Reese, Toni Breidinger, Avantika, and KiiiKiii. Via &Coach, these stars will have the opportunity to share intimate moments in their lives and careers, particularly those centered on transitions and monumental events—or, “the uncertainty before stepping into something new,” as Coach describes them.
Iga Świątek celebrating with dessert after the French Open and PinkPantheress taking stage in Brooklyn are a few of the moments fans will enjoy in this first installment, and to make the most of these scenes, they will be viewed through the lens of notable young directors and photographers like Terrence O’Connor, Sage Adams, and Anthony Po.
Rather than focusing only on building an outward identity with a campaign, &Coach seems to be an invitation to the brand’s base to look inward. In other words, while other brands might offer images focused solely on grandeur and opulence, Coach aims to encourage self-expression and personal creativity through this new model which they call “more than a campaign.” These are virtues that Coach’s Chief Marketing Officer, Joon Silverstein, believes are key to connecting with Gen Z.
“This generation doesn’t want to inherit a prescribed version of aspiration—they want to shape identity for themselves, and they want to be part of the process,” Silverstein says. “&Coach was built by bringing creators, cultural voices, and hundreds of consumers into the work from day one.”
&Coach comes to life today on social media at @and.coach
#CoachPartner #coach #fashion #andcoach

posts.caption:
Tyra Banks isn’t a fan of her villain edit.
The former executive producer and host of ‘America’s Next Top Model’ is suing Netflix over her portrayal in the viral docuseries ‘Reality Check: Inside America’s Next Top Model.’
In a lawsuit filed Saturday, Banks alleges producers used “selective editing, deliberate omission, and surgical manipulation” to create what she calls a “false and defamatory narrative.” She says that of the three-and-a-half-hour interview she gave for the series, only 16 minutes made it in and all the accountability she took “ended up on the cutting room floor.”
The suit challenges several moments featured in the documentary, including Shandi Sullivan’s account of her experience on Cycle 2. Banks argues that Sullivan’s version of events has changed over time and claims that she “would have addressed the situation in a vastly different way,” adding she “had no awareness whatsoever that Ms. Sullivan’s experience in Milan might have involved anything other than a regrettable, but consensual encounter.”
Banks is also upset that producers allegedly failed to tell her that Jay Manuel, Miss J Alexander, and Nigel Barker were not only interview subjects but also credited consultants on the project. According to the suit, that information “would have raised a red flag” and “she would not have participated.”
Another claim Banks calls “the most hurtful example” involves the documentary’s suggestion that she didn’t support Miss J after his stroke. Banks says she has been living in Australia, tried to locate him and has receipts to prove it. She also says she and Miss J have had some contact. “They spoke live on the phone at least once…They texted numerous times.”
The lawsuit essentially argues that Netflix crafted a convenient narrative. Hmm, sounds familiar. Guess there really are “no sacred cows.”
Swipe through for some of the lawsuit’s messiest allegations.
#antm #tyrabanks #tv #fashion #news

posts.caption:
Betsy Ross is turning in her grave.
American flag-inspired looks for the “Octagon Girls,” the official ring card models for the UFC, have been revealed for Sunday’s fight on the White House lawn.
“The inspiration was born from the intersection of sport, spectacle, and celebration,” costume designer Marina Toybina told Maxim. Toybina is a seven-time Emmy winner, winning three alone for her work on ‘The Masked Singer’...lol.
“Working closely with UFC, we wanted to create looks that honored both the energy of the brand and the significance of the 250th anniversary celebration.”
The sparkly looks include hot pants, bustiers, and multiple flag code violations. According to US Code Title 4 Chapter 1, “the flag should never be used as wearing apparel” and “no part of the flag should ever be used as a costume or athletic uniform.”
The images are unsurprisingly being dragged online. “Looks like a failed design challenge on Project Runway,” wrote one Threads user. “Tacky outfits for a tacky event being hosted by a tacky administration,” wrote another.
This weekend’s Freedom 250 fight will take place on Trump’s 80th birthday, which also happens to be Flag Day.
#flag #costume #news #threads #viral

He went on to describe a recurring cycle in which Marshall would “berate” workers and “scream at them” before periods of non-payment. 

“And that’s on top of grueling hours, up to 17 hour days, and up to 100 hour work weeks,” the District Attorney added.

Marshall is alleged to have withheld payment for 924 work hours accrued by nine workers. Not only did she fail to pay them for these hours, but she withheld compensation even after the workers obtained small claims judgments. Text messages show one of the workers begging Marshall for payment, explaining how she needed to send funds to Ecuador for her sick son.

Marshall’s misconduct is not isolated to garment production workers, but extends to contractors and freelancers who worked with Salon 1884 such as “modeling agencies, a pattern maker, and a hair stylist.” She also neglected to make payments to worker’s compensation insurance.

Marshall, despite yesterday’s court proceedings, posted six times on her personal Instagram to promote a Salon 1884 online archive sale. Ironically, Salon 1884’s website touts “exceptional craftsmanship and sustainable practices” and says that garments are “responsibly made with environmentally-conscious materials and manufactured in The United States in New York City’s Garment Center.”

Guess it just goes to show you really can’t assume anything about pricing equating to fair labor in fashion!

posts.caption:
EXCUSE????? What are the men using????
The modern man has come a long way from hunting wild boar and gathering berries. His greatest prey now is a Korean exfoliating acid with a cult following and a waiting list. Once upon a time men changed governments. Now they change skincare routines.
Pigmentation, sebum, enlarged pores, fine lines. The enemies of the state have changed. Somewhere along the way they discovered that looking handsome adds considerable weight to an opinion. Mansplaining, but with luminosity. A little easier to digest when delivered through glass skin. Every evening, across the world, men stand in front of mirrors dragging serum droppers across their foreheads with the concentration of Renaissance painters restoring a chapel ceiling.
Nothing feminine about looking good, darling. Vanity has always been a masculine pastime. The Greeks knew it. The dandies knew it. The peptide-using gymshark-wearing gym bros accidentally rediscovered it.
No homo. Just metro. Metrosexual. A term so gloriously of its time it deserves preservation in a museum.

posts.caption:
Arjun in Zindagi Na Milegi Dobara was a portrait of the workaholic millennial (are they okay??). It was meant to expose the absurdity of a high-profile corporate life that demanded constant availability. The kind of life tethered to every corner of the world and dictated by schedules across time zones. Watching it in 2011, you thought: who joins a client video call in the middle of a road trip? You nodded along with Laila, “Insaan ko dibbe mein sirf tab hona chahiye jab woh mar chuka ho.”
Do you laugh at your own naïveté now that your vacation has turned into eat, pray, Teams?
Once a physical location, the office is now something far more elusive. Stepping out of an office door meant shutting shop for the day. Work was confined by actual walls. But now that your phone is practically your conjoined twin, clocking out feels more like a corporate myth. Physical accessibility is no longer a limitation. Whether you’re on a beach in the Maldives or at a breakfast buffet in a Hyatt, any place can become a conference room if you put your mind to it. The only holiday epiphany you’re likely to have is the realisation that somewhere between your employment contract and your inbox, you signed your soul and calendar away.
DISCUSS ☕️✍🏽

posts.caption:
Wait, I was about to type “generate visuals if Ex Machina was set in Old Delhi” into my ChatGuPTa, but VegNonVeg had already sent me these 🕹️💅😌
A collaboration between the OG Indian sneaker concept store and the brand that made audio-tech f*cking cool. VNV turns 10, marking a decade of giving Indian sneakerheads (you know who you are) a dedicated space at the dawn of hypebeast culture. With their new Beats collaboration, they’ve tapped into a category I’d call editorial headphones.
Because let’s be honest, it has never been just about sound quality. It matters how headphones look on a person. By integrating Indian silver jewellery, a craft tradition with its own sonic history, they’ve created the accessory for the tech generation.
Everyone wish VegNonVeg a happy burrrdayyy! 🎂

posts.caption:
S is for Sarasa Textiles.
The label was founded by designer Maribel George after she found herself “unable to let go” of the work she was doing as part of the Venkatagiri Revival Project. They understand that one cannot claim to champion craft remotely from plush air-conditioned offices in tier-one cities, and has instead chosen to root itself in Venkatagiri itself. Here, they study the environment in which the cotton is woven, the women who wear it, and the myriad ways it is draped.
In a commercial landscape, where brands move from one emotional-support craft to another within a matter of months, Sarasa’s collections are developed over a span of up to four years, shaped by rigorous experimentation, historical inquiry, and deep textile study. Think Kanchipuram, Madras checks, and ikat enriched with exquisite zari, all woven in the quiet luxury (complimentary) of non-starched cotton, with saris ranging between ₹12,000 and ₹40,000.
Their campaigns, much like their saris, are strikingly contemporary yet steeped in nostalgia. They evoke the feeling of returning home. Like leafing through an old family photo album and finding elegant women in beautiful saris nestled between rain-soaked landscapes and images of lovingly prepared meals.
Sarasa might be making the most exquisite cotton saris, but what’s stands more brilliant is the complete abandonment of the fast modern fashion supply chain and still being able to be much more desirable than most bigger brands the market has to offer.

posts.caption:
#ELLEDigitalCoverStar: There is a version of Kirandeep Chahal’s (@kirandeep_chahal) backstory that will hit close to home for many Indian women. To come from a family that cannot comprehend the desire for a life earned and lived on your own terms. To be expected to get married young, start a family, and follow a pre-written narrative folded neatly into the same shape it has always taken. Then there is the version she followed instead, one that eventually led her to becoming a familiar fixture on the runways of Dior, Dries Van Noten and Rick Owens, among other names that make fashion disciples starstruck.
Head to the 🔗 in bio to read the cover story.
On @kirandeep_chahal: LOVE ring in white gold & diamonds, LOVE bracelet in white gold, sapphires, & diamonds, LOVE bracelet in rose gold, pink sapphires & diamonds, LOVE bracelet in yellow gold, tsavorite garnets & diamonds, all by @cartier. Mala top by @raw_mango. Miss Z Riviera heels by @christianlouboutin.

posts.caption:
#ELLECoverStar: There is a version of Zinnia Kumar (now Rose) (@zinniakumar) that fashion understands easily. The model. An image that moves seamlessly between Paris, New York, and wherever the industry gathers next.
It is also the least interesting version of her.
We’re at Le Bristol in Paris and the day carries all the familiar markers of a luxury cover shoot. Controlled light. Precise framing. Cartier anchoring the narrative. But Rose doesn’t perform that script in the expected way. She arrives ready, slips into each look without negotiation, barely checks the monitor. There is no sense of overworking the image, only an instinctive understanding of how to move within it. Head to the 🔗 in bio to read our conversation with the cover star.
On @zinniakumar: Clash de Cartier earrings in rose gold and green dyed agate, Clash de Cartier necklace in rose gold, Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and chalcedony, Clash De Cartier ring in rose gold and onyx, and Clash de Cartier ring in rose gold and red dyed agate, all by @cartier. Textured draped top paired and draped trousers both by @newbottega

posts.caption:
#ELLEDigitalCoverStar: For Chaitnya Sharma (@_slowcheeta_), Mumbai remains his biggest creative influence. Not the glossy, cinematic version of the city, but its contradictions. The glamour and the grief. The ambition and the exhaustion. “There’s nothing like Bombay in the world,” he says. “The deeper you go into the city, the more you realise it contains everything: dreams, crushed dreams, rejection, acceptance, riches-to-rags stories, rags-to-riches stories.” That layered chaos has shaped his music as SlowCheeta. Songs that sound easy and playful on the surface often carry personal truths underneath. He points to ‘Bada Ghar Chhota Ladka’ (2020), a track many assumed was a flex anthem. “But it actually came from a much more traumatic and emotional place,” he explains. “I think my music works like Bombay itself. It packages deep emotions in a chill sonic space. The more you invest in it, the more there is to discover.” More at the 🔗 in bio.
On @_slowcheeta_: Vandalism jacket by @aestheticofresistance. Barrel leg jeans by @vegnonveg. Leather deck shoes, Stylist’s own

posts.caption:
Presenting our first of four Summer Issue cover stars, @annehathaway.
Due to filming and release delays, Hathaway now has five movies premiering within six months. Her insanely busy year continues with her starring role as Penelope in @theodysseymovie. (To which she says: “It’s crazy to have four years of work coming out in six months.”) After weathering the storms of Hollywood, the in-demand star is finally collecting her flowers—and stopping to smell them. “If I’m not enjoying it, then really, really what’s wrong with me?” she tells ELLE. “If I can’t have a positive outlook on it when I’m having such a fortunate ride, then it would be wasted on me.”
For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Hathaway reflects on how she’s grown since the early days of her career, reuniting with Christopher Nolan after 12 years, and navigating online criticism: “We’re at a time when people feel very confident in assuming what they think is fact.” Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
In one simple question, @bella_freud gets some of the world’s biggest stars to open up: “Can you tell me what you’re wearing and why you chose those particular clothes?”
Since 2024, with her podcast @fashionneurosis_bellafreud, that single question has bypassed PR-trained responses, unlocking personal histories usually reserved for therapy. It’s how she’s coaxed shocking vulnerability from guests like Dior creative director jonathan.anderson, @lorde, and more. That’s the magic of Bella Freud—or, as she sees it, the magic of fashion. “I don’t see clothing as armor. I want it as a conversation starter,” she explains.
For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, @veroniquehyland speaks to Freud about her family history as a great-granddaughter of Sigmund Freud, how she cultivates intimacy with her guests, and whom she considers fashion’s greatest storytellers. Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
When Tini Younger began her culinary education in 2021, money was tight and her kitchen was cramped. The night she uploaded her first cooking video to TikTok, she had 1,800 followers; when she woke up the next morning, the video had over 14 million views. And thanks to an incredibly viral mac and cheese recipe and an appearance on Gordon Ramsay’s ‘Next Level Chef,’ she has now amassed just as many followers across social media—and earned enough to buy her own home last year, at age 23, outside of Augusta, Georgia, where she now lives with husband (and onscreen food reviewer) Antoine Wright Jr. and their infant daughter.
“I was able to make myself a beautiful, green kitchen,” Younger says. “It’s really insane how the internet can do that.”
Younger sees teaching others to cook as her way of paying it forward; she’s done it for years in her videos, and now she’s gearing up to do it more formally with the publication of her first major cookbook, ‘Today We Are Cooking: Recipes That Teach You to Think Like a Chef.’ At the link in bio, the chef opens up to ELLE’s @kaylaw about going viral—and what to expect from her next step.

posts.caption:
Father’s Day means something different when your kids have four legs and zero boundaries. Gurfateh Pirzada (@gurfatehpirzada) has two of them, Berlin, named after his favourite Money Heist character and Sydney, named after Sydney Sweeney, and they have taken over his home, his mornings and his plate. Berlin wants what he is eating. Sydney wants what Berlin is eating. Gurfateh just wants to finish his coffee in peace.
We spent a morning with the three of them ahead of Father’s Day and the dogs had the final word on everything.
Photography: Dinesh Ahuja (@dinesh_ahuja)
Videography: Baasil Khan (@baasilkhan)

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover
posts.alt_text:
💕💚After I went on Lorraine last year I made a silent promise to myself that I would be back at the ITV studios and I had my eyes set on @thismorning. I can’t believe just a few months later, this promise became a reality.

Of course I was low key stressing 😅😅 but there was absolutely zero reason to be nervous because the entire (and I’m not exaggerating) team were so warm and lovely that I felt like I was hanging with friends all day. 

Firstly thank you for everyone that tuned in and sent good luck vibes🍀💖! A huge shout out to @lilaflintroberts @sarah_barlow  and @amberstyledit for the opportunity to shine a light on #fashionpsychology and for allowing me to flex my styling skills. 

💃🏾🔥I couldn’t have asked for better models to showcase those amazing looks. @kate_mitchell78 @jazzifizzle53 and @misskspence you KILLT IT 

And last but certainly not least the pros @hollywilloughby and @alisonhammond55 thank you for making me feel so comfortable, it was an honour to watch you both work! 

Stay tuned for a reel on all of the BTS goss but for now I’m in Estonia about to speak on the biggest stage I have so far. Wish me luck! 🍀💖 

Dress: @fumithelabel 
Shoes: @asos 

#Tv #fashionpsychology #press #itv #thismorning

posts.caption:
Poised, purposeful, and unapologetically ambitious, Devita Saraf has spent decades charting her own course. A self-made entrepreneur with an unwavering belief in her vision, she embodies a new kind of power, one that balances confidence with grace and success with authenticity.
The Hermès sari she wears tells a story of its own—a dream she held onto for years, that was waiting for the right moment. A reminder that some aspirations aren’t just achieved, they’re aligned.
Photographer: @taras84
Stylish: @surbhishukla
Set Designer: Sets by J, @artnut_j
Makeup & Hair: @eleni_chatzinikolidou
Saree: @Hermes
Jewellery: @jaipur_jewels
Heels: @jimmychoo
#DevitaSaraf #Femina
posts.alt_text:
For over six decades, Femina Miss India has been more than a pageant. It’s woven into the cultural fabric of India. It is a platform that has consistently celebrated ambition, individuality, resilience, and purpose, highlighting the inherent beauty of our women on the global stage. 

This year’s winners are Femina Miss India World 2026 Sadhvi Sail, Femina Miss India 2026 – 1st Runner Up Rajnandini Pawar, and Femina Miss India 2026 – 2nd Runner Up Dr Sree Advaita, each representing modern Indian women. Together, they embody the spirit of Femina Miss India: connected by purpose, united by ambition, and ready to shape the future on their own terms.

(On the cover from L to R - Rajnandini Pawar, Sadhvi Sail, Dr Sree Advaita)

Photographer: @vaishnavpraveen - @thehouseofpixels 
Styling:@divyakdsouza
Makeup: @sonamdoesmakeup 
Hair: @mikedesir (Agency: @animacreatives) - hair assistant @safalta_b 
Nails: @anishaamulchandani, @studionails_mumbai
Agency for photography, styling, makeup and nails: @theartistsproject 
Styling Assistant: @bhairaviahuja @panchi.bezbaruah

From L to R
Black pants: @rajeshpratapworks
Black corset: @byolakh
Black dress: @namratajoshipura\
Black skirt and top: @rudrakshdwivedi
Jewellery: all @tanishqjewellery

#Femina #FeminaCover #FeminaMissIndia

posts.caption:
"Today, I believe that power, blended with poise, should be an innate part of your personality. Even if someone meets you outside of a business or social setting, they should be able to sense that you are a powerful person. That inner sense of power develops over many years — when you've taken risks, built something meaningful, been a leader, influenced people, and learned from your journey. It becomes something no one can take away from you."
Photographer:  @taras84
Stylish: @surbhishukla
Set Designer: Sets by J: @artnut_j

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠
posts.alt_text:
Meet the top rated brands of 2025 🎊⁠
⁠
In the past year, we rated more than 1500 brands and are proud to share the ten that received our highest marks, illustrating a commitment to more sustainable practices that protect people, the planet, and animals 💚⁠
⁠
What’s a theme that unites these brands? 👀 Outside of a commitment to reducing their impact, these brands are all small and independent, a reminder of where to look in the industry for leadership and innovation. If you’re looking for a reminder to support small brands this year, this is it.⁠
⁠
Cheers to all these brands, along with all the brands that were rated “Good” and “Great” in the past year! Here’s to continuing to make a difference and for championing a better approach to what we wear. Find the full list at the link—and share which brands you’re hoping to see rated in 2026 in the comments 💬⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #SustainableBeauty #EcoBeauty #Beauty #BeautyBrand #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion

posts.caption:
What can be accomplished in ten years?⁠
⁠
A decade may seem short but, in reflecting on all the work we’ve done in the past decade, we’re in awe of everything that has transpired—and are grateful for the hard work of the current and former team members along with you.⁠
⁠
Whether you’ve shared our posts or have been a partner in this business, the work of creating a more sustainable future isn’t done alone but in partnership with others. These accomplishments are an example of what happens when likeminded people come together in the hopes of a better world 🌍🌎🌏⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn about ten things Good On You has accomplished in the past ten years. Share how you think the sustainable fashion and beauty industry have shifted in the past decade 💚⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Since 2025, like many things, Pride hasn’t been the same as attacks on diversity, equity, and inclusion have meant less support for the LGBTQ+ community.⁠
⁠
This may seem like a siloed political situation, but know that such a problem ripples into how communities are treated—and the poor treatment of workers in supply chains. How? Diversity and sustainability intersect at a community level, resulting in dehumanizing situations like Loro Piana paying hundreds of dollars for animal fibres while greatly underpaying workers, all for sweaters that cost almost $10K USD.⁠
⁠
By disempowering vulnerable people and communities, economic power is removed. Your clothes are a representation of this, which is why it’s integral to support brands that not only support communities like the queer community but who are doing right by workers. These actions go hand-in-hand.⁠
⁠
Companies and leadership quitting Pride is worrisome as it relates to freedoms but also that it ripples into businesses rolling back progress, creating covers for waste, economic inequality, gender and sexual disparities, and other harmful business practices. Remember this, when the need arises to make a purchase 👀⁠
⁠
Here’s to making change that benefits all communities, and to a happy Pride 🏳️‍🌈 Learn more about these issues and how problems like pinkhushing turn back the clock of progress⁠ ↑⁠
⁠
#Pride #LGBTQ #Pride2026 #Sustainability #SustainableFashion

posts.caption:
Ultra fast fashion is bad enough. But, when you add in right wing money, the situation gets far worse 😧⁠
⁠
As @bof points out, ultra fast fashion brand Cider is backed by venture capital money. When you follow that money, you very quickly and very easily can find connections to some very, very bad billionaires, whose relationships tie to everyone from Donald Trump to Jeffrey Epstein.⁠
⁠
This may seem disconnected from fashion but, when you consider how brands like Cider have built their successes on the exploitation of people, the planet, and animals, such a sordid business model and such dirty money make sense together. Yet another reason to avoid ultra fast fashion—and spread the word too 📣⁠
⁠
Swipe to learn more about the connection between Cider and right wing billionaires. What are your thoughts on this news? Share in the comments and tag a friend to keep them in the know. Visit the link for an explainer on the dangers of ultra fast fashion ↑⁠
⁠
📸 Business of Fashion⁠
⁠
#ShopCider #CiderGang #SHEIN #Sustainability #Fashion⁠

posts.caption:
Fashion loves dirty energy that pollutes the planet 😮‍💨⁠
⁠
In a recent @fash_rev report, a damning—and unsurprising—revelation came out that the fashion industry is obsessed with emission-heavy practices given so few brands disclose coal phase outs, the electrifying of factories, or scaling renewable energy. Here’s what that looks like in specifics —⁠
⁠
🔥 Only 18% of brands are phasing out coal⁠
🌬️ Only 11% of brands reveal renewable energy in supply chains⁠
🪧 Only 7% are advocating for renewables in the countries where their clothes are produced⁠
⁠
This isn’t just an issue of planetary pollution but that the local communities where garment workers live and work are becoming toxic, all as brand profit, banking on your not-caring about the suffering they’re enabling. Shameful 😖⁠
⁠
This is a reminder to consider the impact of fashion beyond something like wasted textiles and clothing recycling: the impact of the industry goes way beyond the clothing itself. Visit the link to read the full report and send this post to a friend to help them understand why fashion needs to solve these problems ↑⁠
⁠
#Sustainability #Fashion #FashionNews #SHEINFind #Zara⁠
