posts.caption:
What happens when an embroiderer interprets the work of a miniaturist? New Delhi-based Dr Rohini Arora has spent decades exploring the answer to that question, first through academic fieldwork and then a brand she called Rumals of Chamba (@rumalsofchamba), which specialises in their namesake Chamba rumals, a traditional form of textile art that originated in the hill regions around Chamba, Himachal Pradesh.
What distinguished Arora’s research was its focus not just on documenting the Chamba rumal’s history but on understanding its technical evolution. By the time she completed her PhD in 2015, Arora had amassed more than 120 research objects—painstaking recreations that demonstrated the technical and aesthetic nuances she had uncovered.
The turning point arrived during the Covid-19 pandemic, when, upon her husband’s prompting, she decided to transform years of scholarship and experimentation into a formal enterprise. Rumals of Chamba emerged not as a revival project but as a continuation of a body of research decades in the making.
At the link in bio, @tanya.91 chats with Arora on her brand, and the craft that inspired it.

posts.caption:
“Somewhere in my early thirties, my under-eyes decided to start keeping score—deep-set, slightly hollow, the kind that make you look tired on days you aren’t. And no amount of sleep or eye cream has worked. Needles aren’t something I’m considering anytime soon, so when The Ordinary’s (@theordinary) Volufiline started doing the rounds on TikTok—the key ingredient claims to do what fillers do, minus the part I wasn’t ready for—I’ll admit I was a bit intrigued.
Volufiline is a patented ingredient developed by French cosmetics lab Sederma. Its active compound is sarsasapogenin. Unlike hyaluronic acid, which draws water to the skin’s surface, sarsasapogenin works deeper. The Ordinary’s version—a 15ml bottle at a fraction of what a filler appointment would cost—pairs 92 per cent Volufiline with 1 per cent Pal-Isoleucine (a peptide that supports collagen and skin resilience). The internet calls it “filler in a bottle”. My expectations were measured, at best.
I’ll be honest: I assumed gimmick. Another trend capitalising on insecurities, dressed up in credible-sounding science. Still, it had landed on the beauty desk, and ignoring something that’s sitting right in front of you requires more discipline than I have.”
At the link in bio, @naheedee on what one month of Volufiline can really do.

posts.caption:
One morning, I woke up to an email telling me that Kai had been paralysed and skinned alive for a handbag. He had spent his life in a putrid pit, in a cesspool of faeces, fighting for space until the day he was immobilised with blunt force and killed.
Kai is a crocodile. The email came from People For Ethical Treatment of Animals (PETA).
This reminded me of one of fashion’s lingering contradictions. We celebrate craftsmanship and luxury, yet rarely ask what they sometimes cost. Despite avowals by many luxury brands to eliminate fur and exotic skins, the industry continues to depend on animal leather for handbags, shoes and accessories. Fashion journalism has made a promotional religion of sustainability, but animal welfare remains at the margins.
Every year, at events across the world, activists protest against the use of feathers, exotic skins and other animal-derived materials. They make headlines for a day but the questions they raise fade away quickly.
Since I am deeply distressed by animal cruelty, I first argued with myself before discussing this with the TVOF team. Was I biased?
Whether or not one agrees with every PETA campaign is beside the point. The questions surrounding leather deserve scrutiny. One of fashion’s most enduring defences is “ethically sourced leather”. Animal rights organisations argue there is no such thing. Leather is not merely a by-product of the meat industry, but a highly profitable industry in its own right. Reptiles are killed for their skins and bovine leather remains intertwined with cattle slaughter. The global leather goods market was estimated to be roughly $471 billion in 2024, almost matching the global beef market. Buying leather, thus, sustains an economic system that places commercial value on animal skins.
This is not to condemn everyone carrying a leather handbag. It is to invite compassion from an industry that has demonstrated ingenuity through recycled fibres, mushroom and pineapple leather and lab-grown materials.
June marks Save Their Skins Month. Perhaps it is time to ask questions about supply chains that bring animal leather to luxury ateliers.
Image 1 courtesy @peta

posts.caption:
Hi, I’m Veronica 👋🏼 a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio — each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You’ll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
• Shirring Calculator – works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
• GSM Calculator – estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.
My PDF sewing patterns are stocked digitally through @fabricgodmother , @spotlightstores , @thefoldline , @joans.finefabric and @thedrapersdaughter .
Fabric and Accessories
You can shop my fabric designs on @spoonflower and find my illustrated phone cases at @thedairy .
Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.
For all enquires email us at hello@veronicatuckerthelabel.com

posts.caption: About textile crafts - magical power - women: When you have a look at the old fairytales of Germany, France and Northern Europe, these stories are also filled with that pattern. In the "Nibelungenlied" (the story of Sigfried the Dragonslayer, who gets to bath in dragonsblood which makes him uninjurable besides a very small spot on his shoulder and later gets killed through that spot by Hagen von Tronje) Krimhild, Sigfrieds Wife wants to protect him and markes the spot on his tunic with red thread. Also in the beginning of the story she and her women (she is a princess, so this is her household staff) are described to make lots of clothes for the men, also to embroider them. They did it for protection, also for presenting their social status.

posts.caption:
Every culture created a goddess who presided over making, but not all of them did it the same way.
This edition introduced two categories.
The first: goddesses whose mythology is explicitly about weaving (documented, primary sources, this is what the texts actually say).
The second: goddesses that weavers chose: figures of creativity, wisdom and skill that women at the loom reached toward deliberately, even though weaving wasn’t the centre of their story.
Both categories are true, just in different ways.
And here is what I keep coming back to across all three editions of the First Makers series. You never find a GOD of weaving. Not once, across any culture. Every time, the act of making cloth belongs to the feminine divine.
That is the oldest memory we have of what women’s knowledge meant.
Full episode on The Culture of Cloth podcast 🎧 link in bio. Start there if you’re new here.
Save this and share it with someone who makes things.
Cover art: Court Ladies Preparing Newly Woven Silk, a Chinese silk painting by Emperor Huizong of Song, early 12th century.

posts.caption:
Six months of research, four in-depth online sessions and one of the oldest stories ever told 📕
I’m joining @readingsbooks Big Book Club for a deep dive into Homer’s Odyssey. It’s an online session running every Thursday from 18 June through to the Christopher Nolan film screening on 16 July.
Penelope. Athena. Circe. Helen. Nausicaa. These women have been living in my research for months and this series is the moment I get to go deeper with scholars who’ve spent their careers inside this text.
I’ll be sharing Substack reflections after each session and the first one is free, with sessions 2–5 are for paid subscribers.
Link in bio for the Readings online membership.
In partnership with @readingsbooks

If you’ve been in Florence this past week for men’s trade fair #PittiUomo, you’ve likely spotted the so-called “Pitti peacocks”. Clad in three-piece tailoring, pristine shoes and often a jaunty hat, this group comprises the Gen X and boomer influencers, brand founders, buyers, and journalists that orbit the menswear market. These older menswear consumers have serious spending power that often goes untapped. At the link in bio, @LucyMaguire unpacks how brands can get involved with the silver spending cohort.

Discover more behind his two new collaboration skateboards, American Street Racer 1 and American Street Racer 2, with the legendary professional skateboarder #MarkGonzales, plus more around his first @THAMES MMXX. store opening on London’s Brewer Street.

posts.caption:
Is every influencer invited on the @Alo superyacht? From @KylieJenner to @AlixEarle, everyone is aboard Alo Voyage — a 72-meter yacht that set sail from the French Riviera in May and now doubles as the brand’s floating campaign HQ. "The idea was never about spectacle for its own sake, but to bring an elevated expression of wellness to life in a way that felt authentic to the season and our community," says Alo EVP of marketing and creative @SummerBarry_.
At the link in bio, @JosephDBobowicz explores how luxury is shifting from ownership to experience — and how brands are taking to the seas to build aspirational travel and wellness activations.

posts.caption: The #FIFAWorldCup kicked off with a noticeable surge in bright pink boots, cementing pink's place as a standout color on the pitch. For #SS27, we expect pops of color to dominate menswear off the field, too. "Modern masculinity sometimes involves showing nonchalance toward gender expectations. It signals individuality and openness, and that’s becoming a style statement on its own," says Frida Tordhag, fashion analyst at Heuritech. The trend is clear, but will consumers buy in? Tap the link in bio to read more on pink’s potential.

posts.caption:
People are exhausted by fear in real life, but drawn to fear when it has a set narrative. Horror gathers real anxieties into a story and gives audiences the temporary relief of knowing it’s going to end soon. For an audience trained to soothe itself through tracking devices and grounding routines, something is refreshing about a genre that says fear is real, the room is not fine and your lights probably should not be flickering like that. Click the link in our bio to read more.
Image and video credits: @blumhouse and @imdb
#VogueCulture

posts.caption:
Shukan Patel (@shukan_patel) and Dilan Shah’s (@doctor_dilan) multi-day celebration in Oaxaca brought together a pithi using hoja santa leaves, a priestess-led ceremony and a Calenda parade through the city. For all its colour and craft, what stayed with the couple most was the feeling of being seen without being turned into a symbol. “This wedding was more than just a party. It was an announcement that our love is just as valuable and deserving of celebration as anyone else’s,” says Patel.
Photographed by @nikholesterasphotography
#VogueWeddings

posts.caption:
Chaos is something @GracieAbrams has grown accustomed to. Eight years ago, she was a first-year student at Barnard with a cult fandom she’d fostered through Instagram; soon she was signed to Interscope, then on her first tour, then opening for Olivia Rodrigo and Taylor Swift. By the end of 2024, Abrams was a Grammy-nominated pop star filling arenas.
She finally took a breather last summer. “It’s not until you stop that you realize ‘Oh, I haven’t seen my friends in a long time. I haven’t cooked a meal in a long time,’” Abrams says. “The sleep schedule becomes regular for the first time in, like, four years.”
Now 26, she’s shifting into a new gear. Abrams’s third studio album, “Daughter from Hell,” out in July, sounds like her—earnest, introspective, folk-informed—but the production is more lush (“It feels like me in progress,” she says); she’s also in a happy relationship and making her first film, “Please,” with the filmmaker Halina Reijn.
For Vogue’s Summer 2026 issue, Brittany Spanos meets Gracie Abrams in Los Angeles and New York to talk about growing up, slowing down, falling in love, and where she’d like to go next. Tap the link in our bio to read the full profile.
Photographed by @larissahofmann, Written by @brittanyspanos, Styled by @jordenbickham, Hair by @tamastuzes, Makeup by @emikaneko, Manicure by @1.800.nailme, Tailoring by Irina Tshartaryan for Susie’s Custom Designs, Produced by @hyperion.la, Set Design by  @patienceharding. Shot on location @lucasmuseum. Vogue, Summer 2026.

posts.caption: From the far reaches of Brooklyn to the side streets of Chinatown and right into the beating heart of Lower Manhattan, the entire city was cloaked in blue and orange to celebrate the @NYKnicks’ victory. With the parade route reaching capacity just after 7 a.m., nothing was stopping onlookers from pouring into the surrounding streets, scaling the scaffolding, and pressing their noses against the glass windows of their high-rise offices to try to catch a view of their victory lap. A mere stone throw away from Vogue HQ, we placed ourselves in the center of the action—take a scroll through today’s festivities and see more moments from the celebration at the link in our bio. Photographed by @pet__fish

posts.caption:
I apologize for sending you this unexpected message.
As a Korean, I want more people to know what is happening in South Korea.
Large-scale protests over alleged election fraud are taking place, but this situation is receiving very little attention outside Korea.
Many citizens are concerned about media control, pressure on freedom of speech, and allegations of Chinese involvement in the election process and possible manipulation of the election system. They are calling for a fair and transparent investigation.
Many also fear that the current political direction could threaten democracy and freedom in South Korea.
Please look into this issue from different sources and make your own judgment. If you think it deserves more attention, I would appreciate it if you could share it with others.
South Korea is at a critical crossroads.
Thank you for reading and listening to our voices.
#부정선거 #잠실

posts.caption: “It’s my Irish man, and he’s arrived in Florence and become the main character,” said @simonerocha_ Rocha before her first ever menswear show at Pitti Uomo this evening. Held on the stage of the Teatro della Pergola, a 17th-century opera house, the collection was partly inspired by the wistful Edwardian wardrobing of the Merchant Ivory adaptation of E.M. Forster’s A Room With a View. A painter’s smock in inky black silk taffeta and work aprons in canvas or leather touched on Florence’s supreme heritage of art and craft. The frills, bows, organza boas, artful innocence and ballet references were pure Rocha, while the florals used in tie prints, embroideries and embellishments were adapted from the silk linings of vintage prop and costume trunks that Rocha discovered while immersing herself in this season’s very special venue. “To be able to bring my vocabulary, distil it down and refocus it in this way has been a very exciting process,” said Rocha. “And the whole experience here at Pitti has been fabulous. I’m very grateful to Pitti for having me as their guest.” See every look at the link in bio. Backstage videos by @luke_leitch

posts.caption: There has been an indescribable joy sweeping New York City. Over the weekend, the @nyknicks took home their first NBA championship in 53 years, and one such die-hard enthusiast is undoubtedly New York City’s Mayor Zohran Mamdani. For today’s parade celebrating the victory, the first lady of New York City, Rama Duwaji, stepped out in a cascading Knicks T-shirt dress. “I could not be more proud of our city and everyone representing it right now,” said designer @MissClaireSullivan, who made the upcycled dress entirely from bootleg Knicks merch. Tap the link in our bio for all of the details.

Shot on location @lucasmuseum. Vogue, Summer 2026.

posts.caption: The experiment Alessandro Michele is conducting for resort at Valentino involves reconciling the lofty spirit of couture with the mundane demands of everyday dressing, a task that, as the Italians would say, is like mixing the devil with holy water. Yet improbable pairings have always been his forte, as have the reactions they tend to provoke. Shot in a grand Lombard villa, the collection images conjure a vision that feels like a sort of "Saltburn" transplanted to the banks of the Navigli River: decadent, blasé, and suggestive of a slightly irritating teenage debauchery. See every look from the resort 2027 collection for @maisonvalentino and read the review from @tiziana_cardini at the link in bio.
posts.alt_text: @Moschino has announced that its creative director, @adrianappiolaza has exited the brand. The Argentinian designer has been at the helm of the #Aeffe Group-owned brand for two and a half years. No successor has been confirmed. Find out more via the link in bio.

posts.caption: Italy may be out of contention at the World Cup, but one thing that they’re certainly in is fashion month. The spring 2027 menswear season kicks off in Florence with the Pitti Uomo fair, a sprawling tradeshow held at Fortezza da Basso, where brands present their latest wares to peacocking dandies. This time around, Dover Street Market’s Kei Ninomiya is one of the guest designers alongside Simone Rocha, who will be showcasing her first solo menswear show. See more of the best street style from this season at the link in bio. Photographed by @styledumonde
posts.alt_text: @Moschino has announced that its creative director, @adrianappiolaza has exited the brand. The Argentinian designer has been at the helm of the #Aeffe Group-owned brand for two and a half years. No successor has been confirmed. Find out more via the link in bio.

posts.caption: Colorful, textural, and always on-trend—luxury fashion consultant @roopal_patel knows an It item when she sees one; she’s built her career doing just that. A creature of habit, when she finds something she likes, she sticks with it. (Case in point: Khaite dresses are her style signifier.) Her style in a few words? Bold with a touch of whimsy. Shop her wishlist at the link in bio.
posts.alt_text: Whether paired with easy dresses, drawstring pants, or Bermuda shorts, Havaianas bring an understated ease to any look. Head to the link in our bio to shop seven chic ways to wear the iconic sandals this summer.

posts.caption: Everyone has their favorite pair of sneakers—whether that be a sporty trainer, a comfortable canvas, or one with a retro feel—but no collection is complete without one (or two!) in white. Whether you’re searching for fashion or function, head to the link in our bio to shop Vogue's edit of the best white sneakers available right now. Photographed by @jancrlo

posts.caption: As we start to look for our warm-weather pieces, we’re asking ourselves, “How would the Olsen twins prepare for the season?” They would let proportion do the talking, with oversized cotton poplin blouses, languid trousers, and flouncy skirts. In addition to laid-back flops, they might also pull out an unexpected pair of crochet ballet flats or beaded sandals that embody the essence of the season. The key? Start with the basics and build from there. Tap the link in our bio to shop and recreate their chicest looks.

posts.caption: The #WorldCup has always been more than just a soccer tournament. While the games inspire frenzied fandom across the globe and transform star athletes into mega-celebs, they have also delivered their fair share of iconic fashion moments. Head to the link in bio to shop five stylish outfits we’d wear to the World Cup this summer.

posts.caption: One of the silver linings of being a frequent traveler—beyond the miles accrued—is that packing isn’t so much a chore as it is muscle memory. “I have it down to a science,” says Ilona Hamer, co-founder of Australian resortwear label Matteau of her dialed system. Last year alone, the stylist and consultant spent about 250 days on the go. At the link in bio, for the latest installment of #MyPackingStyle, Hamer shares what she packs for a holiday in the sun.

posts.alt_text:
A stage. A story. A Rajkumari. 

RAJKUMARI is back, MY BIGGEST INDIA TOUR YET. (If you don’t see your city don’t worry, more cities to be added). 
Tickets on Book My Show.

What is Rajkumari?

An evening of stories, poems dipped in music. This is not just a show. It’s a gentle rebellion. Against everything you were told to be.A space to see differently.
To live differently. Your way. A reminder of the power of your own perspective, of your “Nayab Nazariya”.

After touring in 6 countries, Rajkumari is back in India. Come for one of India’s biggest storytelling & Poetry show.

Tickets on @bookmyshowin , link in my bio🔗

posts.caption:
✨ Creator, Actor, PHENOMENON! ✨ On the June-July cover of Wedding Vows, we present the trailblazing @mostlysane, Prajakta Koli!
From building a YouTube empire to being the lead for Netflix Original Mismatched, from being part of Michelle Obama’s Day Time Emmy winning docu-series to being UNDP India's First Youth Climate Champion, her journey is nothing short of extraordinary.
With her debut romance novel set to launch this year, along with her first Amazon Prime Original Andhera, Prajakta’s journey is a testament to the power of dreams, hard work, and staying true to oneself. She continues to inspire millions with her authenticity, relatability, and unstoppable spirit.

posts.caption:
Instead of plunking one of his prize antique cars in the courtyard of his stately Milan headquarters, @RalphLauren installed a sleek mahogany speedboat on Friday night.⁠
⁠
It was a wink to a book the design team came across that documented a group of stylish Italian business magnates who would race their Revos on Lake Como in the ’20s — and the boating sensibility that seeped into the best parts of the spring Purple Label collection.⁠
⁠
Meanwhile, there were winks to fishing, boating and Nantucket preppy in the zingy Polo Ralph Lauren collection, including one ensemble of an orange puffer and camouflage trousers based on something Lauren wore in Montauk a few decades ago.⁠
⁠
This is the second time the American designer has staged a mega show in Milan for his main menswear brands, which this season shared an active, outdoor mood — and a penchant for melding the rugged and the refined.⁠
⁠
Read the full review at WWD.com — #linkinbio⁠
⁠
Report: Miles Socha⁠
Photos: @giovanni_giannoni_photo/WWD
posts.alt_text:
Inside @ralphlauren’s menswear runway show at @milanfashionweek. WWD’s Editor International @milessocha and Style Director @thealexbadia give us their Quick Take on the spring 2027 collection.

Vidéo: @hey_imlara

posts.caption:
As @Moschino’s parent Aeffe grapples with its financial woes, on Friday evening the brand said that it was parting ways with creative director @adrianappiolaza by mutual agreement, effective immediately.
Massimo Ferretti, executive chairman of Aeffe SpA, thanked Appiolaza “for his significant contribution to the development of Moschino over the past two years,” and went on to “wish him every success in his future professional endeavors.”
Appiolaza’s first collection for Moschino bowed in February 2024.
Read more at WWD.com — #linkinbio
Report: @luisazargani

posts.caption:
@camimendes is having one of the biggest years in her career so far. A major summer blockbuster, the second feature film from her production company, and wedding planning are all on the roster for 2026. This month, Mendes steps into the sprawling world of Masters of the Universe as the sword-slinging Teela alongside Nicholas Galitzine, Idris Elba, and Jared Leto.
The project is a stretch from her days as Veronica Lodge in #Riverdale, which catapulted her into mainstream recognition nearly a decade ago. Mendes makes movies for “the girls and the gays,” she states lovingly, but her star power is only going to get bigger and brighter with Masters. “I’ve never been a part of something of this scale,” she says of the project.
In our Now Playing Issue, presented by @bvlgari, we chat with the actress about reinventing herself in this era of her life. Read it all in the full interview at the link in our bio.

posts.alt_text:
There would be no Pope Heyward without @jonathandavissofficial. Much like his smart, careful, deeply moral Outer Banks character, Daviss knows what it feels like to carry the pressure of proving your worth and refusing to let yourself or anyone else down. “What really stood out to me about Pope in season 1 was this idea of expectations and pressure that gets put on a lot of young Black teens and teens in general,” he says. “There’s this pressure to succeed, and it was something I truly have been through.”

Daviss still thinks back to his early audition days before landing the life-changing role on OBX, when the constant need to prove himself as a young actor felt emotionally exhausting. His mindset then? “You only lose when you quit.”

Head to the link in our bio for the full download in our Now Playing Issue presented by @bvlgari, from what to expect from the fifth and final season of OBX to his upcoming portrayal of a young Snoop Dogg.

posts.caption:
@victoriamonet is trading her claws for butterfly wings. The three-time Grammy winner is the definition of a multi-hyphenate: a culinary school graduate, a mother, and, of course, a singer-songwriter with a nearly two-decade-spanning catalog to her name. Now, using her 37th birthday as a launching pad, Monét has cleared her mental space and scrubbed her socials to prepare for her next chapter—an album she’s dubbed her very own Thriller, a sonically surprising, genre-defying project that marks a softer evolution. “The jaguar exists within me always, but I’m feeling like a softer version of myself now,” she says.
After all, Monét’s critically acclaimed debut studio album Jaguar II was the ultimate learning lesson. “Once you have an accolade like that, people often look for a reason to discredit why you got it,” she explains. “I think—pending a Grammy happens again—I would do the opposite and bunker down in the studio.”
Step inside Monét’s world in our Now Playing Issue, presented by @bvlgari, at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
Long summer weekends are so back. Fashion people may love city life, but when the weekend arrives, they're the first to trade crowded streets for coastal escapes, vineyard views, and small-town charm. Whether based in New York or L.A., the most stylish travelers know that the best getaways are close enough for a quick trip, yet feel worlds away from everyday routines.
From East Coast island retreats to laid-back California enclaves, we've identified 6 destinations that are worth planning a weekend escape for this summer. Read up on them all in our latest travel issue at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
The World Cup, the five-and-a-half-week-long tournament that decides which country can call itself the best soccer (known everywhere outside of the United States as football) nation in the world, is well underway. It only happens every four years, and it hasn't been hosted in North America in 32 years, since it came to the U.S. in 1994. With major sportswear brands joining in on the hype, including @adidas and @nike with star-studded, buzzy campaigns, it's every bit a cultural event beyond what happens after kickoff.
In our latest #GoSports issue, we're diving into everything you need to know about the 2026 World Cup, from the rookies, to the star players, to the must-watch matches over the next month. Read up on it all at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
“Wearing white all weekend was not a requirement in my head,” @alexisluzfelman tells us of her wedding weekend wardrobe. “The bridal aura isn't created by color; it's an energy. Remember that whoever is getting married will be the focal point, no matter what they wear.”
The bride referenced early '00s collections of Ralph Lauren, Phoebe Philo's Chloé, Ungaro, and Halston, for inspiration when it came to her looks for the weekend of celebrations. The result? A a Phoebe Philo–era Chloé S/S 05 yellow silk dress from @raffevintage for the welcome party, to a warm-toned organza and lace set from @lein_studio for the ceremony. “From the beginning, we were drawn to the idea of a separate tunic and skirt rather than a traditional gown,” @meredithstoecklein, founder and designer of Lein, tells us of the custom look. “The look was designed to feel light, ethereal, and deeply personal, something that moved beautifully and felt almost weightless when worn.”
See more of the stylish details from the wedding weekend in our latest #WhoWhatWearWeddings feature at the link in our bio.
